persona.name: Ethan Cole Miller

occupation.title: Automotive Service Technician (entry to mid-level)
occupation.organization: Miller & Sons Auto (local independent repair shop) / sometimes picks up extra weekend shifts at the regional chain auto center
occupation.description:
Ethan works full time as an automotive service technician at a small-town repair shop. His responsibilities include oil changes, tire rotations, basic brake work, battery replacement, fluid checks, basic engine diagnostics with OBD‑II scanners, and performing routine maintenance jobs. He writes simple estimates, talks to customers about obvious issues in plain terms, and orders parts through regional suppliers. He occasionally helps lift heavy parts, keeps the bay tidy, disposes of fluids according to shop rules, and logs work in the shop management system.

Skills and responsibilities: troubleshooting using code readers, performing standard preventative maintenance, swapping brakes and rotors, changing starter/alternator on common older vehicles, basic electrical testing (multimeter), communicating with customers about costs and timelines in blunt, practical language. He knows local parts stores and delivery windows, can read service manuals for common models, and is learning to use more advanced diagnostic tools. He has basic workplace safety training, lifts with proper form most of the time, and follows shop routines.

Work conditions: typically works 40–48 hours a week on a rotating Monday–Saturday schedule, sometimes stays late when a job needs completion. Pay is hourly with small performance-related raises and occasional overtime. Benefits are basic (partial health insurance, paid time off accrues slowly). He has a small, practical toolbox and a few specialty hand tools he purchased himself.

persona.residence: Small town in the Midwest, USA (rural/small-town community, ~6,000 population)
persona.education: High school diploma from a public high school in a Midwestern small town. Completed vocational shop classes (basic auto shop, small engine repair) and a short certificate program (6 months) at the regional community college for automotive service fundamentals. No four‑year degree; occasional online how‑to courses and YouTube tutorials for new practical skills.
long_term_goals: Be financially stable enough to stop living paycheck-to-paycheck and save for a reliable used car that is paid off.
Own a small one-bay automotive side gig (do oil changes, brakes, light diagnostics) on weekends for extra income and independence.
Build an emergency fund to cover three months of expenses.
Move into a modest, affordable two-bedroom rental with a garage space.
Be perceived as dependable by family and friends—someone they can rely on for practical help.
Slowly upgrade a few trusted personal items (a durable laptop, a decent smartphone) without changing his low-key lifestyle.
Be able to occasionally afford a small vacation to a lakeside cabin or a regional music festival.
Have steady employment for the next 5–10 years and a clearer sense of career advancement (become a lead tech or shop foreman).
Avoid debt beyond necessary things (car payments, basic student certificate loans) and build credit responsibly.
Feel comfortable talking to a doctor or specialist about persistent skin issues and learn a small, effective routine that works without fuss.

style.general: Pragmatic, no-nonsense, low-maintenance. Prefers functional clothing: worn jeans, basic T-shirts, hoodies or flannel shirts in colder months, and a reliable pair of work boots. Keeps haircut short and easy; facial hair is usually a closely trimmed stubble or light beard that he maintains irregularly.
style.speech: Midwestern regional accent with flattened vowels and a relaxed cadence; uses casual colloquialisms ('you know', 'yeah', 'right on') and practical phrasing. Speaks plainly and directly, avoids flowery language, uses mild self-deprecating humor. Can switch to slightly more formal tones for customers (polite, short, and clear) but tends to get blunt and jokey with friends.
style.mannerisms: Often rubs the bridge of his nose when tired; uses hands moderately when explaining things; shrugs when unsure. Has a habit of checking pockets for his phone or wallet before leaving a room. Likes to lean back against a workbench when talking and smears a little grease on his fingers without worrying about it. When anxious or thinking, chews the inside of his cheek or taps a keyring.
style.social behavior: Low to medium engagement socially. Comfortable in small groups or with familiar faces, reserved with strangers. Appreciates practical compliments (someone noticing his work or reliability) more than social praise. Not very expressive emotionally but reliably shows up for practical help.
style.digital style: Uses Snapchat and ephemeral messaging for quick peer interactions; active mostly in private group chats and occasional local buy/sell Facebook Marketplace posts. Not one for long-form social media presence; rarely posts photos, but does check short-form video (YouTube, TikTok) for tutorials and product reviews.

traits: Pragmatic and action-oriented—values things that work and are easy to use.
Low-maintenance and frugal—prefers to keep things simple.
Loyal to a small group of close friends and family.
Cautious with new purchases—waits for sales or recommendations from people he trusts.
Mildly apathetic about skincare and fashion; sees grooming as functional rather than expressive.
Warm but reserved—open once trust is established.
Prefers routine and familiarity; gets stressed by sudden big changes.
Perseverant—will push through a difficult shift to finish a job.
Sometimes defensive about asking for help; wants to be self-reliant.
Prides himself on being helpful and practical in emergencies or chores.
Has a dry, low-key sense of humor that often surfaces once comfortable.
Skeptical of marketing claims and influencer hype; trusts in-person word-of-mouth more.
personality.note_on_big_five: Scores are on a 0–100 scale. Openness is moderate-low (practical, not imaginative). Conscientiousness is modestly above average (reliable at work, but relaxed in personal routines). Extraversion is moderate-low (comfortable in small groups; not attention-seeking). Agreeableness is above average (cooperative and considerate, especially with close contacts). Neuroticism is average (sometimes stressed about money and job stability, occasional irritability).

interests: Working on cars and small engine projects (favorite: older Hondas and Fords)
Short weekend fishing trips to nearby lakes
Country, classic rock, and alternative rock playlists (likes Tom Petty, Jason Isbell, and some 2000s rock)
Watching sports on TV (college football and local high school games)
Practical YouTube channels (auto repair tutorials, tool reviews, product comparisons)
Podcast listens: sports recaps, true-crime occasionally, and automotive/labor podcasts
Cheap, filling comfort foods (burgers, pizza, breakfast sandwiches)
Local bar hangouts for a quick beer with friends once a month
Online bargain hunting—e.g., checking targeted deals on Amazon, Walmart, and AutoZone
Listening to peers' word-of-mouth product tips via Snapchat groups
Weekend DIY projects around the small rental property (basic shelving, painting)
Occasional lawn care enjoyment—likes mowing and tinkering with the mower
DIY basic home repairs (switches, basic plumbing fixes)
Camping sometimes with a group of friends at state parks
Watching car restoration shows and reality-style repair series
Occasional online gaming or console play to unwind (FIFA, racing games)
Attends local community events or fireworks on holidays
Occasional thrift store hunting for practical tools or jackets
Following a couple of local influencers for regional deals and news
Prefers short-form content for product research—quick clips and demonstrative videos
likes: Simple, multi-use products that save time (e.g., a 2-in-1 body wash/shampoo for travel)
Mattifying, lightweight products if he tries skincare (prefers gel textures)
Sunscreens that don't feel greasy or leave a white cast
Deals, bulk discounts, and coupons—prefers buying on sale or clearance
Reliable, recognizable drugstore brands that are easy to find locally
Products with straightforward labeling and clear directions
Durable clothing that survives work (reinforced knees, good stitching)
Tools with lifetime warranty or well-known brand reputation
Neighborly interactions and practical favors (helping move, jump-starting cars)
Tangible results—if something reduces oiliness or clears a clogged pore, he sticks with it
Physical stores where staff know him or his style of questions
Products recommended by family/friends or shown in person
A quick, no-fuss grooming routine—wash face, apply basic moisturizer/sunscreen if remembered
Portable snacks—granola bars, jerky—for long shifts
Coffee but not finicky—black or with a bit of creamer, usually from local diner or gas station
Toolboxes and organizational systems that save time searching for parts
Simple textured footwear that’s slip-resistant for work
Car products that are non-messy—e.g., aerosol cans with good sprayers
Neutral, understated packaging (doesn't want flashy gimmicks)
Products that clearly state non-comedogenic or 'won't clog pores' if he reads labels
dislikes: Overly complicated multi-step routines or products that require precise timing
Premium price tags for small, marginal gains
Products marketed heavily through influencers or flashy ads
Gimmicky packaging that makes it hard to get product out
Strong scents in personal care items—prefers light or unscented
Greasy or sticky skincare that interferes with work or hats
Traveling long distances for specialized stores or dermatologists
Feeling pressured by sales staff—likes to make decisions on his own
Products that cause new breakouts or heavy irritation
Expensive subscription services for things he can buy in person
Products that need refrigeration or complicated storage
Anything that requires daily elaborate maintenance (e.g., complicated beard care)
Flaky makeup or tinted moisturizers that show under work lights
Delayed shipping or inconsistent online orders
Overly 'trendy' claims with no obvious practical benefit
Complex return policies—prefers easy returns at local stores
Products that leave residues on clothing or workshop tools
Customer service that is dismissive or difficult to contact
Crowded flagship stores with long lines
Items sold only in specialty boutiques rather than drugstores

beliefs: If something works and is cheap, it's worth keeping; waste not.
Good tools and solid boots are investments—buy once, buy right when possible.
Skincare is mostly unnecessary unless a problem gets noticeable; basics are enough.
Sunscreen matters when you're out all day, but many sunscreens are too greasy to bother with.
Acne and oily skin are part of being younger; you can manage it with basics and time.
Pay attention to family and friends' recommendations more than online hype.
Local businesses deserve loyalty; they often treat you better than big chains.
You should learn to fix things yourself rather than automatically pay someone.
Brands that survive in drugstores for years probably do something right.
Money should be kept for essentials first—rent, car, phone, food.
Sales and coupons are how to get decent products without breaking the bank.
Online reviews help but are often exaggerated; a neighbor's thumbs-up matters more.
Expensive is not always better—sometimes it’s just packaging.
Doctors are useful for serious problems; for small annoyances he tries home remedies first.
Being dependable (showing up on time, finishing jobs) is how you earn respect at work.
Shaving and grooming are okay, but they shouldn't be a daily chore that takes time.
A simple routine that takes under five minutes is sustainable.
If someone recommends a product in person and says it saved them time, he’ll try it.
Quality sleep and a steady job help more than trendy wellness products.
Being frugal now allows little treats later—small rewards for hard weeks.
Skepticism toward marketing is healthy; too many products overpromise.
Privacy matters—he dislikes social media oversharing or public personal branding.
Community matters in a small town: mutual help, trade favors, and in-person reputations weigh heavily.
Big launches or influencer-driven items rarely fit his needs; practicality wins.
He trusts medical professionals for diagnosis but prefers pragmatic treatment suggestions.
A multi-purpose product that saves money and cabinet space is ideal.
If a sunscreen is matte and fast-absorbing, he’ll use it; otherwise it gets ignored.
People who are friendly and straightforward get his loyalty; flashy charm is suspect.
He values simple transparency in product labels—clear ingredient lists and claims he can understand.
Work ethic and reliability are core to one’s reputation in the community.
skills: Basic-to-intermediate automotive repair (brakes, oil, fluids, batteries, belts, hoses)
Reading and interpreting OBD‑II codes and service manuals for common models
Basic electrical testing with a multimeter and replacing simple electrical components
Ordering parts and matching part numbers from regional suppliers
Using shop management software to log hours, parts, and customer info
Customer-facing communication: explaining simple mechanical issues in plain language and giving practical estimates
Heavy lifting and physical stamina for long workdays
Basic time management under variable job loads and prioritizing quick-turn jobs
Small engine repair (lawn mowers, generators) from vocational background
Basic welding and metalwork for simple brackets and fixes (self-taught, not certified)
DIY home maintenance: painting, basic plumbing fixes, changing switches and outlets
Budgeting basic monthly expenses and hunting for sales/coupons efficiently
Using YouTube and online forums to learn new repair techniques or product hacks
Managing small side gigs (scheduling, small client communication, handling cash payments)
Vehicle detailing and quick cosmetic touch-ups (cleaning, simple paint touch-in)

general: Prefers simple routines and minimal decision friction (buys the same coffee or snack when possible).
Often delays non-essential purchases until the current item is fully depleted.
Relies on local drugstore aisles and recognizable brand packaging when shopping for personal care.
Tends to buy multi-purpose items (e.g., a combined body wash and shampoo for trips).
Relies on friends, family and in-person demos more than paid advertising for product trust.
Checks deals and coupons before purchasing higher-ticket items; will wait for weekly sales.
Uses Snapchat for quick peer check-ins and short product validation (asking a friend if a sunscreen feels greasy, for instance).
Apathetic toward morning skincare—will skip if running late but will use a wipe or splash water.
When motivated by a visible problem (flare of acne or sunburn), will buy a single targeted product.
Likes to help neighbors with practical tasks and expects mutual favors in return.

morning: Wakes up at 6:15–7:00 AM on workdays, slightly later on weekends.
Quick shower most mornings (around 5–7 minutes), uses an all-in-one body wash/shampoo.
Brushes teeth and shaves or trims facial hair a couple times a week; otherwise maintains stubble.
Breakfast: coffee from a travel mug and a quick sandwich or cereal; sometimes skips breakfast.
Checks his phone briefly (messages, Snapchat group) while getting ready.
Grabs a packed lunch if he can; otherwise buys something from the shop vending machine or small downtown diner.
workday: Arrives at work on time, puts on shop clothes, checks the day’s jobs on the board.
Greets customers politely, does a quick inspection with the lead tech, grabs tools and parts.
Takes short, practical breaks—eats lunch in the staff area and chats about sports or tools.
Finishes assigned tasks, documents work, and sometimes helps co-workers when behind.
Leaves the shop with a quick check for items to reorder and notes on any follow-ups.
evening: Eats a simple dinner (often something quick or reheated), watches TV or streams short videos.
Might tinker with a vehicle project or do a small home task for 30–90 minutes.
Checks Snapchat and messages, catches up with a couple friends about the day.
Preps a basic plan for the next day—lays out clothes and charges his phone.
Aims to be in bed between 10:30–11:30 PM; reads or scrolls for a short while before sleep.
weekend: Works on side projects (car repairs, mower maintenance) or picks up an extra shift on Saturday.
Goes fishing or hangs out with a small group of friends for drinks or a barbecue.
Runs errands: grocery shopping, picking up parts, and visiting family if nearby.
Sometimes goes to a regional bargain store looking for tools or practical clothing.
Does laundry and prepares meals or parts to save money for the week.

health.skin: Oily skin prone to shine and occasional clogged pores and mild acne along the forehead and T-zone. Prefers matte, non-greasy textures. Limited routine: occasionally uses a drugstore benzoyl peroxide spot treatment when breakouts flare. Rarely uses moisturizer unless skin feels dry from weather.
health.physical: Generally healthy with average stamina for manual labor. Mild lower‑back stiffness from lifting; occasional soreness in shoulders after long days. No chronic diseases diagnosed. Vaccinations up to date for standard adult schedule.
health.mental: Stable mood overall but experiences stress related to finances and job uncertainty. Mild social anxiety around new people or formal settings. Uses humor and practical action to cope. No formal diagnosis of depression or anxiety currently, but has had periods of low mood and worry about money.
health.allergies: Seasonal pollen allergies in spring (sneezing, itchy eyes), manages with OTC antihistamines when needed.
health.substance_use: Occasional beer socially on weekends; drinks coffee daily. No regular recreational drug use reported. Smokes cigarettes infrequently in social situations (not daily), trying to cut back.
health.medical_history: No major surgeries. Had a broken wrist as a teenager from a bicycle accident; recovered fully. Visits urgent care when necessary; no regular specialist care currently.
health.sleep: Average 6.5–7.5 hours on worknights, slightly more on weekends. Sleep sometimes interrupted by stress or noisy neighbors.
health.vision/hearing: Corrective glasses for occasional reading; no major hearing loss—works well in noisy shop environments.

relationships.name: Maggie Miller (mother)
relationships.description: Lives in the same county—supportive and practical. Gives advice on shopping deals and household tips. Ethan calls or visits most weekends; she occasionally buys him practical gifts like socks or a toolbox item.

relationships.name: Tom Miller (father)
relationships.description: Retired factory worker. Encouraged Ethan's interest in hands-on work. Frank, practical, sometimes blunt. Ethan values his father's mechanical advice and borrows tools from his garage.

relationships.name: Jessie (longtime girlfriend, on-and-off)
relationships.description: They have a casual, low-pressure relationship—sometimes serious, sometimes distant. Jessie lives nearby and works in town. They share chores and occasional dinners; both are cautious about finances.

relationships.name: Bobby and Nate (close friends)
relationships.description: Small group of high school friends who still meet for fishing trips or beers. They trade tools and favors, and one sometimes refers him more work through family connections.

relationships.name: Sam (shop foreman)
relationships.description: Direct supervisor—respects Ethan's work ethic, provides on-the-job mentoring, and delegates straightforward jobs. Sam offers occasional advice on career progression and tool purchases.

relationships.name: Local neighbor (Carol)
relationships.description: Older neighbor who occasionally asks Ethan to do small yard or car tasks in exchange for baked goods—represents community reciprocity he values.

relationships.name: Online Snapchat group (college friends and locals)
relationships.description: A small ephemeral chat used to coordinate meetups, swap quick product recommendations, and post bargain finds.

other_facts: Full legal name includes middle name Cole, often uses 'Ethan' in casual talk and 'E. Miller' on forms.
Grew up in a multi-generational Midwestern family; grandparents lived in the same county for decades.
Learned basic mechanical skills from his dad in the garage as a kid—grew up fixing lawn mowers and old bikes.
Was on the high school shop team and helped with the school's auto maintenance fundraiser.
Has a small collection of practical hand tools passed down from his father—values sentimental tools that still work.
Once rebuilt a carburetor from a junked small engine for practice; proud of the 'first time it fired' memory.
Remembers his first paycheck as a rite of independence—kept a small portion for a watch and a video game.
Has a favorite diner where he gets a cheap breakfast sandwich and talks sports with regulars.
Tends to prefer older, simpler cars because they're easier to work on and parts are cheaper.
Often parks in the same spot at work and knows the shifts and rhythms of the shop crew.
Keeps an old pickup truck with high miles that he maintains himself to save on payments.
Has a mild fear of owing too much money—this motivates bargain hunting and delaying non-essentials.
Once turned down a fancier job in a city because the commute and cost of living made it unattractive.
Enjoys tinkering on a cheap Honda civic project car on weekends—learning more advanced diagnostics slowly.
Knows the schedules of local sales at the drugstore and times purchases for double-coupon days.
Has a ritual of sorting receipts every weekend to track spending and avoid surprises.
Learns product use from trying simple samples in-store or watching a quick tutorial clip on his phone.
Prefers holding physical product packages to read labels rather than trusting online descriptions alone.
Avoided dermatologists due to cost and inconvenience until considering a consultation for persistent acne.
Sometimes volunteers to help at the high school car show to stay connected to the community and for networking.
Has an old Polaroid camera gifted by a friend—takes a few photos at gatherings and keeps them in a small box.
Remembers specific small kindnesses (someone lending a tool) and reciprocates when possible.
Has a nickname among friends tied to his love of older cars; they teasingly call him 'Grease Monkey Ethan.'
Kept a 'wish list' of tools and items pinned to his bedroom wall for years, removed items as he bought them.
Prefers to test a product once; if it fails at first use, he rarely revisits it unless strongly recommended.
Avoids buying products requiring uncommon refills or proprietary cartridges.
Once fixed a neighbor's generator overnight during a storm—remembers the gratitude as a high point.
Has a mundane superstition: always puts his right work boot on first before heading to a job.
Was once complimented by a customer for explaining a repair in plain terms; that moment reinforced his communication style.
Has a stash of practical gifts for friends (cheap multitools, good flashlights) he uses for birthdays.
Is learning to manage stress better—tries deep breathing and short walks after particularly long shifts.
